known_issues.insight:
# S21 Immersion Cohort Analysis

## Behavioral Baseline

This 4-miner S21 immersion cohort exhibits severe instability with an average hashrate of only 37.4% over the last 7 days and 113 offline events across 181 readings. The cohort shows extreme variability (0-104.4% hashrate range) indicating intermittent hardware failures rather than consistent performance degradation. Thermal performance is actually excellent with average chip temps of 63.8°C and maximum of 72°C—well within normal liquid-cooled parameters. The 109 action flags over 7 days (60% of all readings) combined with persistent offline status suggests this cohort has fundamental hardware reliability issues rather than environmental problems.

## Common Failure Modes

• **Complete system failures with no PDU access** - Primary failure mode affecting 192.168.188.231 consistently across 25 LLM analyses, indicating PSU or control board death rather than recoverable software issues

• **Intermittent connectivity loss** - 113 offline events in 7 days suggests network controller instability or power delivery problems causing periodic dropouts

• **Restart resistance** - Multiple mentions of "restart failure" and "cannot recover remotely" in recent analyses indicate hardware-level failures that don't respond to firmware interventions

• **Mixed restart outcomes** - LLM reports show "mixed results" from restart attempts, suggesting some units are on the edge of hardware failure while others may still be recoverable

• **Control board instability** - Repeated references to "bad PSU or control board" issues across multiple analyses point to a cohort-wide vulnerability in these critical components

• **No thermal stress patterns** - Zero temperature-related failures despite being flagged 109 times, confirming hardware rather than environmental root causes

## Restart Effectiveness

Based on the LLM analyses showing persistent flagging of the same miners (particularly 192.168.188.231) across 25 scans, restart effectiveness appears very low for this cohort. The repeated mentions of "restart failure" and "cannot recover remotely" indicate that standard firmware restarts are not addressing the underlying hardware failures. This cohort shows classic "restart fatigue" where the same units require repeated intervention without lasting improvement, suggesting the root causes are physical component failures rather than software glitches.

## Recommended Action Bias

For this hardware-compromised cohort, the on-site LLM should use these adjusted thresholds:

- **HR threshold for flagging restart: 60%** (vs fleet default 80%) - This cohort's baseline is already compromised, so flag earlier
- **Restart attempts before ticketing: 1** (vs fleet default 3) - Hardware failures don't improve with multiple restarts; escalate quickly
- **Temp threshold for action: 84°C** (maintain fleet default) - Thermal performance is not the issue here
- **Cooldown after restart: 60 min** (vs fleet default 20) - Allow more time for unstable hardware to stabilize
- **Immediate physical inspection trigger** - Any miner showing "no PDU access" should skip restart attempts entirely and go straight to maintenance ticket
- **Batch replacement consideration** - If more than 2 miners in this 4-unit cohort fail simultaneously, recommend cohort-wide hardware audit rather than individual repairs

## Outliers Needing Individual Attention

**192.168.188.231** - Definitely needs individual deep-dive as it appears in nearly every LLM analysis as completely offline with no PDU access. This is beyond cohort-pattern issues and represents a complete hardware failure requiring immediate physical inspection and likely replacement.

The other cohort members showing intermittent issues are better explained by the cohort-wide hardware instability pattern and don't warrant individual analysis unless they progress to complete failure like 192.168.188.231.

## Hardware Quality Assessment

This cohort is a prime candidate for procurement review and potential batch replacement. With only 37.4% average hashrate, 60% of readings requiring action flags, and persistent hardware failures that don't respond to standard remediation, these S21 immersion units are not meeting operational standards. The failure pattern (PSU/control board issues rather than chip degradation) suggests either a manufacturing defect in this batch or premature component aging that warrants warranty claims or early replacement to maintain fleet reliability.

## Cross-Cohort Insights

This cohort likely has the highest restart failure rate and lowest effective hashrate in the fleet based on the extreme instability metrics. The 113 offline events in 7 days is probably 10x higher than stable cohorts, making this group a significant drag on overall fleet performance and a maintenance resource sink that justifies accelerated replacement scheduling.

## ROOT CAUSE HYPOTHESIS

This S19J Pro has suffered a catastrophic hardware failure, most likely a failed PSU or control board. The miner went completely offline after the April 2nd restart and has remained unresponsive for over 2 weeks with zero PDU power readings and no network connectivity. The hardware fingerprint shows standard BHB42601 boards with bin-3 chips and 14.4V optimal voltage - all normal specs that shouldn't cause spontaneous failure. Given the sudden transition from 90% hashrate to complete death immediately after restart, this points to a power delivery failure rather than gradual chip degradation.

## EVIDENCE FROM DATA

• **Complete power failure**: PDU power readings show 0.0 kW average, indicating no power draw whatsoever
• **Network unresponsive**: Miner has been offline since April 2nd restart with persistent AMS sync issues showing "miner is ONLINE (verified) but AMS reports offline"
• **Sudden failure pattern**: Last working scan showed 90% hashrate (134.1/149 TH/s) on April 2nd, then immediate death after restart
• **Temperature readings**: All chip temps at 0°C since failure, confirming no thermal activity
• **Pre-failure thermal profile**: Normal operating temps of 70-74°C with proper liquid cooling performance
• **Hardware specs normal**: All 3 boards show identical bin-3 H1CX21BH1E chips with standard 14.4V optimal voltage from autotune
• **No gradual degradation**: Performance history shows normal 95-99% efficiency right up until failure
• **15 LLM observations**: Consistent flagging as "offline with no PDU access" requiring physical inspection since April 13th
• **Multiple restart attempts failed**: Firmware restart on April 7th also failed to recover the unit
• **Normal hashboard data when working**: Final working scans showed balanced 49.5 TH/s across all 3 boards with zero hardware errors

## CONFIDENCE LEVEL

High confidence (90%) that this is a hard hardware failure requiring physical replacement rather than firmware intervention. The complete absence of power draw combined with sudden failure after restart strongly indicates PSU or control board death.

## RECOMMENDED ACTION

1. **Immediate physical inspection**: Dispatch technician to check power connections, LED status indicators, and control board responsiveness. Look for burned components, loose connections, or obvious damage.

2. **Power system diagnosis**: Test PSU output voltages with multimeter - should show 12V standby and ability to deliver 14.4V under load. If PSU is dead, replace with spare APW12 unit.

3. **Control board swap if PSU OK**: If PSU tests good, the control board likely failed. Swap with known-good Bb control board and attempt boot sequence.

4. **Escalate to RMA if both test good**: If both PSU and control board test functional, this may be a motherboard/backplane failure requiring complete unit replacement. Create maintenance ticket for hardware swap.

**Thresholds**: If unit doesn't respond within 30 minutes of PSU/control board replacement, escalate to full hardware replacement. Don't waste time on firmware solutions - this is clearly a hardware death.

## PARALLEL INDICATORS

Review other miners in the s19jpro/BIXBIT cohort for similar sudden power drops or restart failures, particularly units that have undergone recent firmware restarts. Check for any miners showing declining power efficiency or inconsistent PDU readings as early warning signs of PSU degradation. The fact that this unit died immediately after a restart suggests potential power surge sensitivity that could affect other units with aging PSUs.

This miner has suffered catastrophic hashboard failure across all three boards. The logs show repeated "found 0 asic of 126, bad chips 126" errors for chains 0, 1, and 2, indicating complete ASIC communication failure. This pattern strongly suggests either a control board failure affecting the ASIC communication bus, or simultaneous PSU failure causing insufficient power delivery to all hashboards. Given the emergency sleep mode triggered by "5 fatal errors in the last 60 minutes" and the complete loss of all chains simultaneously, this points to a control board or PSU-level failure rather than individual hashboard degradation.

The unit has operated across multiple performance profiles ranging from 429 TH/s to 503 TH/s, with recent operation at 473 TH/s consuming 5676W. Temperature performance appears stable with chip temps consistently in the 67-80°C range, well within liquid-cooled fleet norms. The unit shows **chronic low uptime patterns** - multiple analyses note "0 days" or very short uptimes, suggesting either frequent manual interventions or instability issues.

## COMMON FAILURE MODES

Without aggregate data from multiple units, failure mode analysis relies on the historical pattern of this single miner:

• **Profile Instability**: Unit has operated on at least 3 different performance profiles (429, 473, 503 TH/s), suggesting either manual tuning attempts or automatic profile adjustments due to performance issues
• **Power Consumption Variance**: Power draw varies significantly between profiles (5148W to 6218W), indicating the unit may be sensitive to thermal or stability constraints requiring profile downshifting
• **Status Inconsistency**: Reports of "online but uptime=0" suggest potential firmware or connectivity issues requiring investigation beyond simple restarts

## RESTART EFFECTIVENESS

**Cannot calculate meaningful restart success rates** due to insufficient aggregate data (0 total readings in 7-day window). However, the historical pattern suggests **restart fatigue** - the unit appears to require frequent interventions despite being technically "online." This single-unit cohort shows classic signs of a problematic miner that needs deeper investigation rather than continued restart attempts.

## RECOMMENDED ACTION BIAS

For miner 64407 specifically, the on-site LLM should use **stricter intervention thresholds** due to the established instability pattern:

- **Temp threshold for action**: 82°C (vs fleet default 86°C) - slight buffer given profile instability suggests thermal sensitivity
- **Cooldown after restart**: 30 min (vs fleet default 20) - allow more time for stability assessment

- **Profile monitoring**: Flag any automatic profile downgrades as potential hardware degradation indicators

# COHORT ANALYSIS: S21e XP Hyd (Single Unit Fleet)

## BEHAVIORAL BASELINE

This cohort consists of a single Antminer S21e XP Hyd unit (64407 at 192.168.188.26), making it impossible to establish true cohort-wide behavioral patterns. However, based on the 7-day historical deep dives, this unit demonstrates highly variable operational profiles ranging from 429 TH/s to 503 TH/s, with corresponding power draws between 5148W-6218W. The miner shows frequent restart cycles (multiple instances of near-zero uptime in logs), suggesting either instability or aggressive management intervention. Temperature performance appears solid when operational, typically running 67-80°C chip temps well within liquid-cooled parameters. The unit exhibits profile flexibility but lacks operational consistency, indicating either hardware instability or frequent manual adjustments.

## COMMON FAILURE MODES

• **Frequent Restart Cycling**: Multiple deep dive reports show uptime readings of "0d0h0m0s" or very short uptimes (12h26m, etc.), indicating either chronic instability requiring restarts or overly aggressive restart policies. Evidence: 4 of 7 recent analyses mention short/zero uptime periods.

• **Profile Instability**: The miner operates across dramatically different performance profiles (429-503 TH/s), suggesting either thermal management issues requiring profile downgrades or inconsistent operational requirements. This 17% hashrate variance is excessive for stable hardware.

• **Zero Historical Aggregates**: The complete absence of 7-day aggregate data (all "None" values) indicates either chronic offline status, recent deployment, or data collection failures. This represents a critical monitoring blind spot.

# Miner 53517 (192.168.188.231) Diagnosis

## ROOT CAUSE HYPOTHESIS

This miner is experiencing intermittent hardware failure, most likely a failing power supply unit (PSU) or deteriorating control board. The evidence points to power delivery issues rather than hashboard failure - all three hashboards show similar degradation patterns and the miner cycles between complete offline states and partial recovery. The PSU voltage instability (fluctuating between 14.30V-14.60V in logs) combined with frequent AMS sync issues and complete power dropouts suggests the PSU can no longer maintain stable power delivery under load. This is a classic PSU aging pattern where the unit can boot and run briefly but fails under sustained mining load.

## EVIDENCE FROM DATA

- **Complete power failures**: 588x workerOffline alerts and 335x workerOnline alerts indicate repeated power cycling behavior typical of PSU instability
- **Hashrate volatility**: Extreme swings from 208.2% max to 0.0% min with average only 74.9% - healthy miners don't show this pattern
- **Zero PDU power readings**: Average PDU power = 0.0 kW despite miner being online intermittently, indicating power measurement/delivery issues
- **Synchronized board degradation**: All three hashboards show similar performance drops (Board 0: 49.3 TH/s, Board 1: 49.0 TH/s, Board 2: 49.0 TH/s) rather than isolated board failure
- **Failed restart outcome**: April 2nd restart attempt resulted in hashrate dropping from 178.7% to 0.0% - opposite of expected recovery
- **AMS sync issues**: Multiple "AMS SYNC" warnings where miner shows online but AMS reports offline, indicating communication/power instability
- **Voltage fluctuation in logs**: PSU voltage samples varying from 13.81V to 14.16V under load, showing power supply instability
- **Normal chip temperatures**: All boards running 64-69°C (well below 84°C threshold), ruling out thermal issues
- **No hardware errors**: 0 HW errors across all boards suggests chips and hashboards are functional when power is stable
- **EEPROM data intact**: All three hashboards show proper chip identification and normal bin ratings (chip_bin 3), indicating boards are not damaged

## CONFIDENCE LEVEL

High confidence (85%) that this is a PSU hardware failure based on the power delivery patterns and complete system instability. Additional confidence would come from physical inspection showing PSU fan failure, capacitor swelling, or voltage rail measurements under load.

## RECOMMENDED ACTION

1. **Immediate PDU power cycle**: Perform hard power reset via PDU, wait 30 minutes for full PSU capacitor discharge, then monitor for 2 hours to see if miner stabilizes above 90% hashrate
2. **Collect power telemetry**: If restart fails or miner drops offline again within 24 hours, capture detailed PSU voltage logs during boot sequence and under mining load
3. **Physical inspection priority**: If power instability continues, create maintenance ticket for PSU replacement - this unit shows classic end-of-life PSU symptoms and is unlikely to recover with software interventions
4. **Spare PSU swap**: Given the clear power delivery failure pattern, bypass further troubleshooting and proceed directly to PSU replacement to minimize downtime

## PARALLEL INDICATORS

Monitor other S19JPro units in the same rack or PDU circuit for similar workerOffline/workerOnline cycling patterns, as PSU failures can indicate broader power infrastructure stress. Check miners with recent "consumptionChangeLevel" alerts as these often precede complete PSU failure by 24-48 hours.

The logs show consistent "found 0 asic of 126, bad chips 126" errors across all three hashboards simultaneously, which indicates a power delivery or communication failure rather than individual board degradation. The miner entered "emergency sleep mode" after 5 fatal errors within 60 minutes and has been completely offline since April 15th with 0% hashrate and no temperature readings. The consistent pattern of all boards failing initialization simultaneously points to a shared component failure—either the PSU cannot provide stable power to any hashboard, or the control board cannot communicate with them.

## EVIDENCE FROM DATA

• **Complete system failure**: 0% hashrate for the past 4+ days with no temperature sensor readings, indicating total hardware breakdown
• **Simultaneous board failure**: Logs show all three hashboards (0, 1, 2) reporting "found 0 asic of 126, bad chips 126" at the same time—this never happens with individual board failures

# Mining Guardian Weekly Fleet Report - USA 188 Facility
**Analysis Period: April 6-19, 2026 | 49 Active Miners**

## 1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

**Critical hardware degradation is accelerating across the S19J Pro fleet with PCB=0110/BOM=0020 units failing at 4x the rate of newer hardware.** The fleet is fragmenting into two distinct populations: aging S19J Pros experiencing systematic cascade failures, and newer S21/AH3880 units performing well. Most urgent: 24 miners with PCB=0110/BOM=0020 are averaging only 50.8% hashrate with 891 total flags—this is not random degradation but a hardware batch failure requiring immediate replacement planning. The facility's 3-4 month decommission timeline makes repair economics unfavorable; focus should shift to identifying which units can survive until facility closure.

## 2. TOP 5 SYSTEMIC ISSUES

**1. PCB=0110/BOM=0020 Hardware Batch Failure (Hardware Quality)**
- **Affected**: 24 miners across 8 cohorts (s19jpro cohorts) averaging 50.8% hashrate vs 55.6% for newer PCB=0130/BOM=0010 units
- **Evidence**: 4x higher intervention rate (891 flags vs 204), identical BHB42601 hashboard failures, consistent chip bin correlation (Bin 3 showing 68.3% vs Bin 1 at 152.7%)
- **Action**: Create immediate triage list ranking all 24 units by performance. Replace worst 8 performers within 2 weeks before cascade failure spreads.

**2. Complete Offline Miner Pattern (Hardware/Infrastructure)**
- **Affected**: Miner 53517 (192.168.188.231) consistently flagged across 25+ LLM analyses as "offline with no PDU access"
- **Evidence**: Same IP appears in 80% of weekly scan analyses with identical diagnosis, indicating either dead PSU/control board or PDU circuit failure
- **Action**: Physical site visit this week. This miner has consumed disproportionate attention—either fix permanently or remove from monitoring to reduce alert noise.

**3. AMS Alert System Saturation (Data Quality)**
- **Affected**: 15+ notification IDs generating URGENT alerts, with miners like 53517 and 64407 flagged 47% of all scans
- **Evidence**: Cross-miner correlation shows chronic miners consuming most alert bandwidth, drowning out new issues
- **Action**: Implement alert deduplication rule captured in operator decisions—once per day per problem, stop alerting after ticket creation.

**4. Restart Ineffectiveness on Aging Hardware (Firmware/Hardware)**
- **Affected**: S19J Pro cohorts showing 21% restart approval rate (82/385 attempts) vs newer hardware with higher success rates
- **Evidence**: Cohort analysis shows 33-100% restart failure rates on aging PCB=0110 units, while S21/AH3880 units restart successfully
- **Action**: Update restart protocol—after 3 failed firmware restarts on S19J Pro, escalate directly to PDU power cycle. Stop wasting time on ineffective firmware restarts.

**5. Chip Bin Quality Correlation (Hardware Quality)**
- **Affected**: Chip Bin 3 units averaging 46.3% hashrate while Bin 2 units achieve 57.2%
- **Evidence**: 11 miners with Bin 3 chips show 220 total flags vs better performance in lower bin numbers
- **Action**: Flag all remaining Bin 3 chip miners for proactive replacement—these are systematically underperforming and will fail first.

## 3. PROCUREMENT REVIEW

- **s21exphyd/BIXBIT cohort (2 miners)**: WATCH - 14.0% hashrate concerning despite newer hardware
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/3/0110/4 cohort (4 miners)**: REPLACE - 68.2% hashrate with 38 offline events, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/3/0110/unknown_cool (1 miner)**: REPLACE - 0% hashrate, likely dead
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/unknown_cool (8 miners)**: INVESTIGATE - Zero telemetry, data collection failure
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/2/0110/4 cohort (4 miners)**: REPLACE - 24.2% hashrate, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/4 (9 miners)**: REPLACE - 87.4% hashrate but includes dead miner 192.168.188.231
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/4/0130/4 cohort (5 miners)**: WATCH - 50.2% hashrate but newer PCB=0130
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/4/0110/4 cohort (1 miner)**: REPLACE - 55.5% hashrate, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/4/0130/4 cohort (3 miners)**: WATCH - 64.4% hashrate, newer hardware but poor performance
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/1/0110/4 cohort (1 miner)**: REPLACE - 4.0% hashrate, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/STOCK/3/0110/unknown_cool (1 miner)**: KEEP - 96.7% hashrate, good performer
- **s19jpro/BIXBIT/2/0110/4 cohort (8 miners)**: REPLACE - 65.4% hashrate, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/3/0110/4 cohort (2 miners)**: REPLACE - 14.2% hashrate, PCB=0110 hardware
- **s19jpro/STOCK/2/0110/unknown_cool (2 miners)**: WATCH - 71.3% hashrate
- **s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/4 (2 miners)**: REPLACE - 3.4% hashrate, essentially dead
- **s21exphyd/BIXBIT/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/1 (2 miners)**: WATCH - 14.0% hashrate concerning
- **ah3880/AURADINE cohort (2 miners)**: KEEP - 20.3% hashrate but requires custom profiles
- **s21imm cohorts (2 miners)**: KEEP - Good performance when operational

## 4. LOCAL LLM PERFORMANCE REVIEW

**Strong Pattern Recognition**: The local LLM correctly identified miner 192.168.188.231 as requiring physical inspection across 25+ consecutive analyses from April 12-19, showing excellent consistency in hardware failure diagnosis.

**Appropriate Restraint on Thermal Alerts**: Scan from [2026-04-14T13:01] correctly did not flag thermal issues for miners running 67-73°C, following liquid-cooled fleet guidelines effectively.

**Missed Fleet Context**: Analysis from [2026-04-14T18:16] focused on individual miner issues but missed the systematic PCB=0110/BOM=0020 pattern affecting 24 miners. Weekly synthesis would help identify these cohort-level failures.

**Overconfidence in Restart Recommendations**: Multiple scans recommended firmware restarts for chronically failing miners without considering restart history. Needs access to restart effectiveness data per miner.

**Good Hardware vs Software Distinction**: [2026-04-19T20:42] deep dive on miner 53521 correctly identified PSU voltage instability (14.30V-14.60V fluctuation) as root cause rather than software issues.

**Alert Fatigue Creation**: Repeatedly flagging the same offline miners (53517, 64407) without escalation, contributing to alert noise rather than resolution.

**Missing Economic Context**: Doesn't factor facility closure timeline into repair vs replace recommendations, suggesting fixes for hardware that will be retired in 3-4 months.

## 5. OPERATOR RULE REFINEMENT

**Rule 1 (Post-Restart Ready Check)**: Correctly captures status-based vs time-based waiting. Should be generalized to "Wait for operational status confirmation before next action" across all intervention types, not just restarts.

**Rule 2 (Daily Log Collection)**: Well-defined. Consider adding exception handling for consistently offline miners that cannot provide logs.

**Rule 3 (PCB/BOM Quality Gate)**: Excellent rule capturing the systematic hardware failure pattern. Should add escalation timeline: "<70% hashrate = immediate flagging, <50% hashrate = urgent replacement within 2 weeks."

**Rule 4 (S19J Pro Restart Protocol)**: Correctly identifies restart ineffectiveness on aging hardware. Should specify success rate thresholds: "If cohort restart success <30% over 7 days, escalate to PDU cycle protocol."

**Rule 5 (Fleet Inventory Audit)**: Good catch on missing miners. Should add monthly reconciliation requirement, not just ad-hoc triggers.

**Rule 6 (Alert Deduplication)**: Critical for reducing noise. Should add escalation ladder: "Day 1: Alert, Day 3: Reminder, Day 7: Escalate to urgent, Day 14: Auto-ticket creation."

**NEW RULE NEEDED - Cascade Failure Response**: "When >3 miners in same PCB/BOM cohort fail within 72 hours, initiate emergency assessment of all units in that hardware batch."

## 6. PREDICTIVE WARNINGS

**Miner 64407 (192.168.188.26)**: S21e XP Hyd showing 9.4% average hashrate with 47% scan flagging rate. **Expected failure**: Complete hashboard death within 5-7 days. **Action**: Schedule immediate profile downgrade to extend life until replacement.

**Miner 53477 (192.168.188.36)**: S19J Pro with 30.5% hashrate, flagged 47% of scans. PCB=0110/BOM=0020 hardware. **Expected failure**: PSU failure within 10 days based on cohort pattern. **Action**: Preemptively schedule PDU power cycle this week.

**Chip Bin 3 Cascade**: 11 miners with Bin 3 chips showing systematic degradation. **Expected failure**: 3-4 additional units will drop below 50% hashrate in next 14 days. **Action**: Create monitoring dashboard for all Bin 3 miners, flag proactively at 60% hashrate.

**AH3880 Power Profile Instability**: Miners 54504 and 63940 requiring customPower_9500 with 20.3% average hashrate. **Expected failure**: Power regulation issues likely within 7-10 days. **Action**: Investigate power delivery to this equipment type, may need different PDU circuit.

**Network Infrastructure Risk**: 8 miners showing zero telemetry (s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/unknown_cool cohort). **Expected failure**: Possible network switch or VLAN issue affecting monitoring. **Action**: Physical network verification this week before miners go "dark."

## 7. NEXT-WEEK FOCUS AREAS

**1. PCB/BOM Hardware Triage Priority**: Monitor all 24 PCB=0110/BOM=0020 miners daily. Flag any dropping below 40% hashrate for immediate replacement rather than repair attempts.

**2. Alert Signal vs Noise Ratio**: Focus on NEW issues and cohort-level patterns rather than chronic individual miner problems. Implement one-alert-per-day rule for known problematic miners.

**3. Restart Effectiveness Tracking**: Before recommending restarts, check 7-day restart history per miner. Skip firmware restarts on miners with <30% success rate.

**4. Power Profile Monitoring**: Pay special attention to miners requiring custom power profiles (AH3880 units, some S21s). These indicate hardware stress that may predict failure.

**5. Environmental Correlation Validation**: While HVAC is performing correctly, monitor for any correlation between outside temperature increases and specific cohort failure rates.

## 8. METRICS TO ADD

**Restart Success Rate by Miner**: Track firmware restart, PDU cycle success rates per miner ID to inform intervention strategies.

**Hardware Batch Performance Tracking**: Systematically correlate serial number prefixes (YDFTYN3B, HQDZYN3B, HYDTYN3B) with failure rates.

**Alert Fatigue Metrics**: Count alerts per miner per day, track resolution rates to optimize alert thresholds.

**Economic Failure Cost**: Calculate repair cost vs revenue loss vs replacement cost for triage decisions, especially with facility closure timeline.

**Chip Temperature Trend Analysis**: While current temps are fine, track temperature increase rates to predict thermal stress before reaching 84°C threshold.

## 9. REFINED INSIGHTS

# FINAL MERGED WEEKLY FLEET REPORT — USA 188 FACILITY
**Analysis Period: 2026-04-12 to 2026-04-19 | 33 Miners Analyzed | 58-Miner Fleet | BiXBiT USA Fort Worth TX**

## 1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

**HIGH CONFIDENCE HEADLINE:** S19J Pro PCB=0110/BOM=0020 cascade failure is accelerating with 4x degradation rate versus newer hardware revisions, while three critical miners require immediate intervention. **MEDIUM CONFIDENCE:** Fleet monitoring covers only 57% of physical inventory (33 of 58 miners), creating potential blind spots for failure detection and revenue loss.

The facility is experiencing systematic hardware degradation concentrated in older S19J Pro PCB revision 0110 units averaging 57.3% hashrate versus 66.5% for PCB revision 0130. Three miners face imminent failure (53476 complete death, 63940 hashrate collapse, 64407 restart cycling). AH3880 units require firmware rollback per existing operator rule 8, not hardware replacement. With 3-4 month facility timeline, newer S21 hardware should be repaired, not replaced.

## 2. HIGH CONFIDENCE FINDINGS (BOTH MODELS AGREED)

### **Critical Hardware Failures — IMMEDIATE ACTION**
- **Miner 53476 (192.168.188.153):** Complete operational failure, 0 TH/s, requires physical replacement
- **Miner 63940:** Total hashrate collapse with 232+ GH/s fleet impact despite "online" status  
- **Miner 64407:** 0% restart success rate with chronic restart cycling pattern
- **Miners 53521, 53527:** Confirmed part of PCB=0110/BOM=0020 cascade failure pattern

### **S19J Pro PCB Revision Cascade Failure — SYSTEMIC ISSUE**
- **Pattern:** PCB=0110/BOM=0020 units failing at 4x rate versus newer PCB=0130/BOM=0010 revision
- **Performance Gap:** 57.3% average hashrate (old) vs 66.5% average hashrate (new)
- **Affected Cohorts:** Multiple S19J Pro groups with PCB=0110 showing synchronized degradation

### **AH3880 Firmware Issues — NOT Hardware Failure**
- **Miners 54504, 63940:** Systematic monitoring/performance problems
- **Root Cause:** Firmware instability with intact thermal management
- **Action Required:** Apply existing operator rule 8 (firmware rollback)

### **Fleet Inventory Gap — MONITORING BLIND SPOT**  
- **Confirmed:** 33 analyzed miners versus 58 actual fleet size (57% monitoring coverage)
- **Risk:** 25 miners potentially operating without failure detection

## 3. MEDIUM CONFIDENCE FINDINGS (SINGLE MODEL FLAGGED)

### **Chip Bin Quality Degradation (Claude Only)**
- **Pattern:** Chip Bin 3 units averaging 68.3% hashrate vs Bin 1 at 152.7%
- **Assessment:** Valuable insight but needs verification with actual chip bin telemetry data

### **S21 Hardware Value Hierarchy (Qwen Only)**
- **Disagreement:** S21 series should be repaired, not replaced due to hardware value
- **Assessment:** Economically sound given short facility timeline

## 4. CORRECTED ERRORS FROM ORIGINAL ANALYSIS

### **Error 1 — Fleet Size Inconsistency**
**Original:** "49 Miners Analyzed" and "47 of 49 miners online"  
**Correction:** 33 miners analyzed from 58-miner fleet per locked operator rules  
**Impact:** False baseline for availability calculations corrected

### **Error 2 — S21 Replacement Recommendations**
**Original:** Recommended "REPLACE" for s21exphyd and s21imm units  
**Correction:** S21 series represents newest, highest-value hardware—should be repaired, not replaced  
**Economic Logic:** With 3-4 month facility timeline, repair newer hardware, replace older S19J Pro units

### **Error 3 — Redundant Rule Proposals**  
**Original:** Proposed "84°C threshold rule" and restart escalation as new insights  
**Correction:** These are existing locked operator rules 2 and 4, not new discoveries

### **Error 4 — AH3880 Framing**
**Original:** Framed as "investigation" of firmware issues  
**Correction:** Should directly apply locked operator rule 8 (April 8 firmware regression rollback)

## 5. QWEN ERRORS CAUGHT

### **Error 1 — Miner 53482 Assessment**
**Qwen:** Claimed Claude "omitted miner 53482 from predictive warnings"  
**Reality:** Need current telemetry to verify if 53482 is still degrading or has stabilized  
**Assessment:** Qwen may have flagged based on stale data

## 6. PREDICTIVE WARNINGS — CORRECTED MERGED LIST

### **0-3 Days (CRITICAL)**
- **Miner 53476:** Hardware death confirmed—requires immediate physical replacement
- **AH3880 Cohort (63940, 54504):** Firmware rollback needed within 48 hours per rule 8

### **3-7 Days (URGENT)**  
- **Miner 64407:** Complete restart failure cascade expected within 7 days
- **Miner 53527:** Hashboard connection degradation despite current 101.3% hashrate (flagged 48% of scans)

### **7-14 Days (MONITOR)**
- **PCB=0110/BOM=0020 Cascade:** Expect 3-5 additional failures from this cohort
- **Miners 53480, 53483:** Early degradation patterns (7% and 6% flag rates respectively)

## 7. OPERATOR RULES — GENUINELY NEW

### **NEW RULE — Chip Bin Performance Tracking**
"Track chip bin data (1,2,3,4) in cohort analysis. Bin 3+ units show systematic performance degradation and should be grouped for batch replacement evaluation rather than individual assessment."

### **NEW RULE — Board Serial Batch Correlation**  
"When 3+ miners from same board serial batch prefix (first 8 characters) fail within 48 hours, evaluate entire batch for production run quality issues."

### **NEW RULE — Fleet Inventory Reconciliation**
"When monitored miner count differs from physical fleet size by >15%, trigger immediate inventory audit to identify monitoring blind spots and unreported failures."

## 8. META-LEARNING — WHAT THIS COMPARISON TAUGHT BOTH MODELS

• **Pattern Recognition Hierarchy:** Daily granular analysis excels at individual failure diagnosis, while weekly synthesis captures fleet-wide systematic patterns. Both perspectives are required for complete fleet health assessment.

• **Hardware Value Economics:** Performance thresholds must be adjusted by hardware generation and facility timeline. Newer S21 hardware justifies repair investment that older S19J Pro units do not, regardless of similar performance degradation.

• **Locked Rule Application:** Both models must distinguish between applying existing locked rules versus proposing new insights. AH3880 firmware regression and temperature thresholds are established protocols, not discoveries.

• **Data Reconciliation Critical:** Fleet size discrepancies (33 vs 49 vs 58 miners) create cascading errors in all subsequent analysis. Inventory accuracy is foundational to reliable fleet assessment.

• **Confidence Scoring Necessity:** Agreements between independent analyses represent HIGH confidence findings suitable for immediate action. Disagreements require additional data collection before implementation, preventing costly repair/replace decisions based on single-model recommendations.

operator_rules:
POST-RESTART READY CHECK — Status-Based, Not Time-Based

APPLIES TO: All miners after any restart or power cycle

===============================================================================
WHAT EXISTED BEFORE:
===============================================================================

Old rule was: Wait 20 minutes after restart before taking action.
This was a guess before we could read miner status.

===============================================================================
NEW RULE:
===============================================================================

After any restart or power cycle, wait until miner status reads MINING.

Miner status goes through several stages:
- Starting
- Initializing  
- Auto tuning
- (other transitional states)
- MINING <-- THIS is when we are clear to proceed

DO NOT take further action until status = MINING.
Once status = MINING, we are clear to do what we need to do.

===============================================================================
WHY THIS IS BETTER:
===============================================================================

- Status-based is accurate, time-based was a guess
- Some miners stabilize faster, some slower
- We now read miner status directly, so use it
- No arbitrary 20-minute wait if miner is ready in 5 minutes
- No premature action if miner needs 30 minutes to tune

OPERATOR DECISION: UPDATED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
REASON: We can read miner status now. Soon as it says MINING, we are
clear to proceed. Status-based is accurate; time-based was a guess.

DAILY LOG COLLECTION MANDATORY — Direct from Miners

APPLIES TO: All online miners

===============================================================================
RULE:
===============================================================================

Every online miner MUST get a fresh log collected daily at 1PM.
No exceptions.

CRITICAL: Logs are collected DIRECTLY from the miner, NOT through AMS.
The script direct_collect_logs.py bypasses AMS entirely and connects
directly to each miner to pull logs.

This means:
- No dependency on AMS for log collection
- Faster collection
- More reliable (fewer points of failure)

OPERATOR DECISION: CONFIRMED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
REASON: Yes we still do this every day at 1PM. Directly from miners.

===============================================================================

Flag any miner with PCB=0110 / BOM=0020 showing <70% hashrate for immediate 
replacement consideration. This hardware revision has systematic reliability issues.

CASCADE FAILURE WARNING:
AI predicts EXPECTED CASCADE FAILURE across all PCB=0110/BOM=0020 units within 
14-21 days based on current 4x failure rate versus newer hardware. Plan for 
potential wave of failures, not just individual units.

   - Track cohort average hashrate daily

   - Have replacement units ready or on order
   - Schedule maintenance window for batch replacement
   - Prioritize worst performers for first replacement

4. DO NOT INVEST IN REPAIRS for this hardware revision
   - 4x failure rate means repairs are not cost-effective
   - Replacement with newer PCB=0130/BOM=0010 or different model preferred

===============================================================================
FACILITY CONTEXT (USA 188):
===============================================================================

- This facility is being decommissioned in 3-4 months
- Equipment will be replaced with newer, more current hardware
- S19J Pro mine has ~100 miners total, but only 49 currently in AMS
- Remaining miners being added to AMS within next week
- These S19J Pros do NOT have smart PDUs - PDU cycles require physical site visit

===============================================================================
WHY THIS PATTERN MATTERS (LEARNING VALUE):
===============================================================================

This pattern teaches the AI to recognize:
- Hardware revision differences within same model (PCB/BOM combinations)
- Board-level component quality (BHB42601 boards with ED die chips)
- Chip binning correlation to performance (Bin 3 vs Bin 1)
- Cohort failure patterns (cascade failure, not random individual failures)
- When to flag for replacement vs repair

Even with newer equipment, these signals transfer:
- PCB/BOM revision tracking is critical for fleet management
- Chip bin quality affects long-term reliability
- Cascade failures affect entire hardware batches, not random units

OPERATOR DECISION: APPROVED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
ORIGINAL APPROVAL: "This is the type of pattern detection we want - identifies 
hardware revisions with systematic problems so future customers know exactly 
which units to watch."
CASCADE WARNING MERGED: "AI correctly identified potential cascade failure 
across older hardware revision. More detail always - board manufacturer, 
chip details, serial patterns all added."

S19J PRO RESTART PROTOCOL — Antminer S19J Pro (Aging Hardware)

MINER TYPE: Antminer S19J Pro
MANUFACTURER: Bitmain

RULE: For S19J Pro units with >3 restart failures, skip firmware restart attempts and escalate directly to PDU power cycle or replacement evaluation.

IMPORTANT CONTEXT:
- These S19J Pros do NOT have smart PDUs installed
- PDU power cycles require physical on-site visit
- This facility (USA 188) is being decommissioned in 3-4 months
- Equipment will be replaced with newer, more current hardware

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Restart approval rate only 21% (82 approved / 385 total attempts)
- 33-100% failure rates on S19J Pro restarts
- Firmware restarts are ineffective on aging hardware
- PDU cycles are the only reliable remediation

ACTION REQUIRED:
- After 3 failed firmware restarts → stop trying restarts
- Schedule physical site visit for PDU power cycle
- Evaluate for replacement if PDU cycle also fails

OPERATOR DECISION: APPROVED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
REASON: Correct - aging equipment does require this. No smart PDUs available so physical site visit required. Facility going away in 3-4 months, will update with newer equipment.

FLEET INVENTORY AUDIT TRIGGER — All Miner Types

APPLIES TO: All miner types, all manufacturers

RULE: When analyzed miner count differs from fleet specification by >5 units, trigger complete fleet inventory audit within 24 hours.

CURRENT SITUATION (USA 188):
- AI analyzed: 49 miners
- Actual fleet size: ~100 miners (S19J Pro mine)
- Problem: Not all miners are added to AMS yet
- Expected fix: Within next week

WHY THIS MATTERS:
- Missing miners = unreported failures or revenue loss
- AI correctly identified a discrepancy and flagged it
- This is exactly the type of insight we want the system to catch
- Proves AI can work with partial data and still identify gaps

ACTION REQUIRED:
- When miner count discrepancy >5 units detected → audit within 24 hours
- Verify all physical miners are registered in AMS
- Check for offline/unmonitored units
- Update fleet spec after audit

OPERATOR DECISION: APPROVED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
REASON: This is a great catch. The S19 mine is actually 100 miners but not all are added to AMS yet (fix planned this week). The AI figured out there was a discrepancy with the information it had — exactly what we want it to do.

ALERT DEDUPLICATION — Prevent Alert Fatigue

APPLIES TO: All Mining Guardian alerts and notifications

===============================================================================
WHAT EXISTS NOW:
===============================================================================

- Same alerts can fire repeatedly for the same problem
- Flooding Slack with duplicate notifications
- Hard to see real new issues among the noise

===============================================================================
NEW RULE:
===============================================================================

RULE 1: ONCE PER DAY PER PROBLEM
- Same problem on same miner = ONE notification per day max
- If operator did not fix it, they get reminded once the next day
- NOT repeated every scan, every hour, etc.

RULE 2: ONCE TICKET CREATED = NO MORE NOTIFICATIONS
- After a ticket is created in AMS for a miner, STOP alerting
- Operator will see the issue in AMS tickets
- Prevents duplicate alerts between Mining Guardian and AMS
- Resume alerting only after ticket is resolved

===============================================================================
IMPLEMENTATION:
===============================================================================

Track in database:
- Last alert time per miner per problem type
- Whether a ticket exists for this miner/problem
- Skip alert if: same problem alerted in last 24 hours
- Skip alert if: ticket exists and is open

===============================================================================
WHY THIS MATTERS:
===============================================================================

- Prevents operator alert fatigue
- Real issues dont get lost in noise
- AMS is the source of truth for tickets
- Mining Guardian alerts for NEW issues only

OPERATOR DECISION: APPROVED 2026-04-18 by Rob Fiesler
REASON: Should not get duplicates about the same problem. Once a day
reminder in case not fixed the day before. Once tickets are made, no
notices - will see them in AMS tickets.

# MINER 53521 DEEP DIVE ANALYSIS
**Antminer S19JPro (192.168.188.12) — Serial: HQDZYN3BAJGJF1FMT**

## 1. CURRENT STATE ASSESSMENT

**STATUS: DEGRADED — POST-RESTART MONITORING REQUIRED**

This miner is currently in a compromised operational state:

- **Uptime**: 0d0h0m0s indicates a fresh restart has just occurred
- **Profile**: 154 TH/s @ 818 MHz represents significant underclocking from factory specs (~170+ TH/s typical for S19JPro)
- **Action Flag**: RESTART indicates this unit required intervention due to performance issues

The 154 TH/s profile suggests the miner has been automatically downclocked to maintain stability, which is concerning for an S19JPro that should nominally operate at higher performance levels.

**CRITICAL STABILITY FAILURE DETECTED**

The 24-hour trend data reveals severe operational instability:

- **Hashrate**: 0% across all measurements (min/max/avg) — complete hashrate failure
- **Temperature**: 0°C readings indicate sensor failure or complete operational shutdown
- **Profile Changes**: Despite 32 scan attempts, only the current 154 TH/s profile was successfully captured
- **Pool Performance**: Zero accepted, rejected, or stale shares over 24 hours

This pattern indicates the miner has been offline or non-functional for the majority of the past 24 hours, only recently coming online after the restart action.

**INSUFFICIENT DATA — BASELINE ESTABLISHMENT PHASE**

- **Today's Log**: Not available (collection in progress or miner was skipped)
- **Yesterday's Log**: No historical baseline exists

**Critical Gap**: The absence of both current and historical logs prevents detailed error analysis. This is particularly concerning given the operational issues evident in the performance data. Log collection should be prioritized for this unit.

## 4. RESTART HISTORY ANALYSIS

**RESTART PROTOCOL SHOWS LIMITED EFFECTIVENESS**

Key restart metrics reveal concerning patterns:
- **Success Rate**: 0.75 (75%) — below optimal performance
- **Total Restarts**: 2 previous attempts
- **Recovery Time**: 1.0 scans average

**Assessment**: The 75% restart success rate indicates underlying hardware issues that restarts cannot consistently resolve. The current restart appears to have achieved basic connectivity (status=online) but the miner remains in a degraded performance state.

## 5. HARDWARE FINGERPRINT ANALYSIS

**BOARD-LEVEL PERFORMANCE INCONSISTENCIES**

Board 0 Profile Analysis:
- **Voltage**: 14.826V average (14.46V minimum) — within acceptable range
- **Frequency**: 822.3 MHz average — slightly above current profile (818 MHz)
- **Temperature**: 47.5°C average, 52°C maximum — well within normal liquid-cooled parameters
- **Hashrate**: 46,982 MH/s — contributing approximately 47 TH/s
- **Power Draw**: 1,804W — high consumption relative to hashrate output

**Critical Finding**: Board 0 data shows historical operation, but the incomplete board profiles (data cuts off at "Board 1") suggests potential board detection issues or communication failures with remaining hashboards.

## 6. PREDICTION — NEXT 24 HOURS

**HIGH RISK OF CONTINUED DEGRADATION**

Based on current indicators:

**Likely Scenarios**:
1. **Best Case (30% probability)**: Miner stabilizes at 154 TH/s profile but remains underperforming
2. **Most Likely (50% probability)**: Intermittent connectivity issues continue, requiring additional restarts
3. **Worst Case (20% probability)**: Complete failure requiring physical intervention

**Risk Factors**:
- Incomplete board profile data suggests hardware communication issues
- Low restart success rate indicates persistent underlying problems
- 24-hour operational failure pattern suggests systemic issues beyond software

## 7. RECOMMENDATIONS

### IMMEDIATE ACTIONS (Next 4 Hours)
1. **Priority Log Collection**: Manually retrieve logs from this miner to identify specific error patterns
2. **Board Communication Diagnostics**: Verify all three hashboards are being detected and communicating
3. **Performance Monitoring**: Track hashrate recovery and stability at current 154 TH/s profile

### SHORT-TERM ACTIONS (24-48 Hours)
1. **Physical Inspection**: Given the older PCB version (0110) and chip bin classification (2), schedule physical inspection for:
   - Cable connections to hashboards
   - Power supply voltage stability under load
   - Control board communication interfaces

2. **Profile Optimization**: If hardware checks pass, attempt gradual frequency increases to determine maximum stable operating point

### RISK MITIGATION
- **Cooling Verification**: Despite liquid cooling, verify proper flow and contact given the power consumption vs. hashrate efficiency concerns
- **Power Supply Assessment**: 1,804W draw for ~47 TH/s output indicates potential PSU or board-level efficiency issues

**CLASSIFICATION: REQUIRES PHYSICAL ATTENTION WITHIN 48 HOURS**

This miner exhibits multiple indicators of hardware degradation that software restarts cannot resolve. The combination of communication issues, performance degradation, and restart reliability problems suggests this unit needs hands-on diagnostics to prevent complete failure.

# DAILY DEEP DIVE ANALYSIS: MINER 53504

## 1. CURRENT STATE ASSESSMENT

**Status: HEALTHY with Board 2 Performance Deficit**

Miner 53504 is currently online and operational but exhibiting a clear performance asymmetry. The unit is achieving 95.70% of its theoretical hashrate (135.04 TH/s vs 141.11 TH/s ideal), which is within acceptable operational parameters but below the fleet fingerprint average of 95.0%.

- Temperature: Max 77°C (well within safe operating range)
- Voltage: 14.83V (stable)

The miner is liquid-cooled and temperatures are nominal. Board 2's 84.85% efficiency represents a significant performance gap compared to Boards 0 and 1, both operating at near-optimal levels.

## 2. 24-HOUR STABILITY ANALYSIS

**Performance Pattern: Stable with Persistent Board 2 Deficit**

The 24-hour trend data reveals a consistent performance profile:

**Board-Level Analysis:**
- **Board 0**: Stable at 45.71 TH/s throughout all 31 scans (100% consistency)
- **Board 1**: Stable at 46.14 TH/s throughout all 31 scans (100% consistency)
- **Board 2**: Stable at 33.32 TH/s throughout all 31 scans (100% consistency at reduced rate)

**Critical Finding**: Board 2's underperformance is NOT intermittent—it has maintained exactly 33.32 TH/s across all 31 scans over 24 hours. This indicates a hardware limitation rather than environmental or connection issues.

**Voltage Stability**: All boards maintained 15V consistently across the monitoring period.
**Temperature Stability**: Board 2 actually runs cooler (68°C) than Boards 0 and 1 (77°C and 74°C respectively), confirming the issue is not thermal throttling.

## 3. LOG ANALYSIS

**No Yesterday Baseline Available** - This is the first deep-dive analysis for this miner.

**Today's Log Pattern Analysis:**

**Voltage Tuning Behavior**: The miner executed an autotune cycle starting at 00:28:07, testing voltages from 14.79V to 14.90V before settling on optimal voltage of 14.89V at 00:40:10. This is normal automatic optimization behavior.

**Critical Hardware Detection**: At 00:32:06, the system identified 15 bad ASICs on Board 2:
```
Switch to nonce hashes measurement, bad asics: [(2, 111), (2, 112), (2, 113), (2, 114), (2, 115), (2, 116), (2, 117), (2, 118), (2, 119), (2, 120), (2, 121), (2, 122), (2, 123), (2, 124), (2, 125)]
```

This pattern repeated at 00:37:09, confirming persistent ASIC failures on Board 2. **This is the root cause of Board 2's reduced hashrate.**

**Performance Correlation**: The bad ASIC detection explains Board 2's consistent 84.85% efficiency—losing 15 ASICs from a chain would produce exactly this performance profile.

## 4. RESTART HISTORY

**Minimal Restart Activity**: Fingerprint shows only 1 total restart with 100% success rate and 1.0 scan average recovery time. No restarts occurred during the 24-hour monitoring period. The current 2d3h33m uptime confirms system stability despite the hardware defect.

## 5. CROSS-CORRELATION ASSESSMENT

**Hardware Defect Pattern**: The 15 consecutive bad ASICs (111-125) on Board 2 represents a localized hardware failure typical of:
- Manufacturing defect in that ASIC range
- Thermal cycling damage to a specific PCB section
- Power delivery issue to that ASIC cluster

**Model-Specific Context**: As an S19J Pro with "Older Hardware Revision" per operator rules, this ASIC failure pattern is consistent with aging hardware degradation expected in this fleet segment.

**Temperature Performance**: Despite the hardware defect, Board 2 maintains excellent thermal characteristics (68°C vs 77°C on functional boards), indicating the cooling system is working properly and the remaining ASICs are not thermally stressed.

## 6. 24-HOUR PREDICTION

**Stable Degraded Performance Expected**

Based on the consistent 24-hour pattern, I predict:
- **Board 0 & 1**: Will continue optimal performance (99-100% efficiency)
- **Board 2**: Will maintain current 84.85% efficiency indefinitely
- **Overall Unit**: Will remain at ~95% total efficiency
- **No Further Degradation**: The ASIC failures appear stable; no progressive failure indicators detected

**Risk Assessment**: LOW - The hardware defect is contained and stable. No thermal, voltage, or progressive failure indicators present.

## 7. RECOMMENDATION

**DOCUMENT AND CONTINUE OPERATION**

**Immediate Action: NONE REQUIRED**

This miner should remain in production. The analysis reveals:

1. **Stable Hardware Defect**: 15 failed ASICs on Board 2 are producing a consistent, predictable performance reduction
2. **No Safety Risk**: Temperatures nominal, no thermal runaway, no progressive failure indicators  
3. **Economic Viability**: At 95.70% efficiency and excellent reject rate (0.01%), the unit remains profitable
4. **Excellent Pool Performance**: 333,033 accepted vs 31 rejected shares demonstrates solid network contribution

**Long-term Consideration**: 
- Flag for eventual Board 2 replacement during scheduled maintenance
- Monitor for any change in the bad ASIC count (currently stable at 15)
- If Board 2 efficiency drops below 80%, consider priority maintenance

**Fleet Management Note**: This represents a textbook example of graceful hardware degradation—the miner has self-detected the fault, adapted its performance expectations, and continues reliable operation at reduced but stable capacity.

**Bottom Line**: Miner 53504 is operating exactly as expected for hardware with 15 failed ASICs. No intervention required.

pass_2_claude_weekly_ref.analysis:
# WEEKLY FLEET REPORT — USA 188 FACILITY
**Analysis Period: 2026-04-12 to 2026-04-19 | 49 Miners Analyzed | 325 LLM Scans**

## 1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

**HEADLINE: The facility is experiencing a cascade hardware failure across older S19J Pro hardware revisions, exactly as predicted by AI analysis.** The PCB=0110/BOM=0020 revision units are failing at 4x the rate of newer hardware, with 24 units averaging only 57.3% hashrate and consuming massive operator attention (495 flags in 7 days). Three miners have suffered complete hardware death requiring physical replacement (53517, 53521, 53476), while the newer PCB=0130/BOM=0010 units maintain 66.5% performance. The Auradine AH3880 units are experiencing chronic firmware instability despite excellent thermal management. With facility decommissioning in 3-4 months, this is a classic end-of-life hardware pattern where repair investment doesn't justify the remaining operational window.

## 2. TOP 5 SYSTEMIC ISSUES

### 1. **Hardware Quality Cascade Failure — S19J Pro PCB Revision 0110/BOM 0020**
**Pattern:** 24 miners with older PCB revision averaging 57.3% hashrate vs 66.5% for newer revision. Chip Bin 3 units specifically showing 68.3% performance with 4,710 total flags across 11 miners — an 84-point performance gap versus Bin 1 units at 152.7%.
**Affected Cohorts:** s19jpro/BIXBIT/3/0110/4, s19jpro/unknown_fw/3/0110/4, s19jpro/BIXBIT/4/0110/4
**Root Cause:** Silicon quality degradation in higher chip bins combined with voltage regulation issues on BHB42601 boards
**Operator Action:** Flag entire PCB=0110/BOM=0020 cohort for batch replacement consideration rather than individual repairs

### 2. **Auradine Firmware Instability Despite Thermal Excellence**
**Pattern:** AH3880 cohort shows 30.9% average hashrate with 51.8°C average chip temperature — excellent cooling but complete software breakdown. 9 URGENT alerts across 5 notification IDs indicate multiple simultaneous failure modes.
**Affected Cohorts:** ah3880/AURADINE/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/3 (2 miners)
**Root Cause:** Firmware-hardware incompatibility causing hashrate collapse while thermal management remains perfect
**Operator Action:** Contact Auradine for firmware rollback or emergency patch — thermal headroom exists to push performance if software stabilizes

### 3. **Restart Ineffectiveness on Aging S19J Pro Hardware**

# LEARNING REFLECTION — Claude vs. My Deep Dive Analysis

## SECTION 1 — AGREEMENTS (HIGH CONFIDENCE)

**Critical Failures - Complete Agreement:**
- **Miner 53476 (192.168.188.153)**: Both identified complete operational failure (0 TH/s). Claude: "hardware death requiring physical replacement," Me: "Complete operational failure (0 TH/s, sensor failures)"
- **Miner 63940**: Both flagged total hashrate collapse. Claude: "232+ GH/s fleet impact," Me: "Total hashrate collapse despite 'online' status"
- **Miner 64407**: Both identified chronic restart failures. Claude: "restart cycling pattern," Me: "0% restart success rate with chronic instability"

**S19J Pro PCB=0110/BOM=0020 Cascade Failure Pattern:**
- Both identified older hardware revision systematic degradation
- Claude: "PCB=0110/BOM=0020 revision units failing at 4x rate," Me: "4 units below 70% threshold... CASCADE FAILURE WARNING proving accurate"
- Both flagged miners 53521, 53527 as part of this pattern

**Auradine Firmware Issues:**
- Both identified AH3880 units (54504, 63940) with monitoring/performance problems
- Claude: "firmware instability despite excellent thermal management," Me: "systematic monitoring incompatibility or coordinated hardware failures"

**Fleet Inventory Gap:**
- Both noted monitoring coverage discrepancy
- Claude: "49 miners vs actual fleet spec of ~100," Me: "33 analyzed vs ~100 actual fleet confirms inventory discrepancy"

## SECTION 2 — DISAGREEMENTS

**A) Fleet Size and Coverage:**
- **Claude said:** "49 Miners Analyzed" and "47 of 49 miners online"
- **What I said:** "33 Miners Analyzed" with fleet of 58 miners total
- **Who is right:** I am right. The locked operator rules state "Fleet is 58 miners, liquid-cooled." Claude's "47 of 49" also doesn't match his own "49 analyzed."
- **Claude's error source:** Appears to be using stale or incorrect fleet inventory data

**B) Auradine Firmware Handling:**
- **Claude said:** "INVESTIGATE" and suggested contacting Auradine for firmware rollback
- **What I said:** Suggested diagnostic collection but didn't reference rule 8
- **Who is right:** I missed applying rule 8. The locked rules state "Auradine AH3880 April 8 firmware regression: verdict was ROLL BACK FIRMWARE, not replace." Claude was correct to suggest firmware rollback.
- **Why I missed it:** I focused on the monitoring failure symptoms without connecting to the specific firmware regression rule for this hardware.

**C) S21 Hardware Replacement Recommendations:**
- **Claude said:** "REPLACE" for s21exphyd and s21imm units
- **What I said:** These are newer hardware that should be prioritized for repair over replacement
- **Who is right:** I am right. S21 series represents the newest, most valuable hardware in the fleet. With 3-4 month facility timeline, these should be repaired, not replaced.
- **Claude's error source:** Applied blanket performance thresholds without considering hardware value hierarchy.

**D) Temperature Threshold Rules:**
- **Claude said:** Proposed "ignore Low Delta-T HVAC concerns" and "unless 5+ miners exceed 84°C" as new rules
- **What I said:** Applied existing rule 2 "Do NOT flag chip temps below 84 degrees C"
- **Who is right:** I am right. These are already locked operator rules, not new proposals.
- **Claude's error source:** Didn't recognize existing locked rules, presented them as new insights.

## SECTION 3 — CLAUDE'S KNOWN ERRORS

**Error 1 - Fleet Size Inconsistency:**
Claude reported "47 of 49 miners online" when locked rules specify 58-miner fleet. This creates false baseline for availability calculations and procurement planning.

**Error 2 - S21 Replacement Recommendations:**
Claude recommended "REPLACE" for s21exphyd/BIXBIT units, which are the newest, highest-value hardware. This violates economic logic for short-term facility operations.

**Error 3 - Auradine Rule Ignorance:**
While Claude correctly suggested firmware rollback, he framed it as investigation rather than applying the specific locked rule 8 about the April 8 firmware regression.

**Error 4 - Redundant Rule Proposals:**
Claude proposed the "2-restart escalation rule" and "84°C threshold rule" as new insights when these are already locked operator rules 4 and 2.

**Error 5 - Incomplete Predictive Warnings:**
Claude omitted miner 53482 (known canonical degrader) and missed 53476's new pool code 1003 issue, focusing instead on miners with higher flag rates but less critical failure modes.

## SECTION 4 — NEW TECHNIQUES TO INTERNALIZE

**1. Chip Bin Quality Correlation:**
Add chip bin tracking (1,2,3,4) to identify silicon quality patterns. Claude showed Bin 3 averaging 68.3% vs Bin 1 at 152.7% - an 84-point performance gap I completely missed.

**2. Board Serial Batch Analysis:**
Track first 8 characters of hashboard serials to identify production run quality patterns. This could predict cascade failures before they manifest.

**3. Alert Deduplication Metrics:**
Measure scan-to-flag ratios (Claude: "53527: 782/1622 scans = 48% flag rate"). This quantifies chronic problem miners vs. acute failures.

**4. Hardware Revision Performance Baselines:**
Establish different performance expectations by PCB revision. Claude's "PCB=0110 at 57.3% vs PCB=0130 at 66.5%" shows hardware revision matters more than individual miner variation.

**5. Cohort-Based Procurement Decisions:**
Group miners by hardware characteristics (PCB/BOM/Chip Bin/Cooling) for batch replacement decisions rather than individual assessments.

**6. Firmware vs Hardware Failure Differentiation:**
For miners with good thermal management but poor hashrate, investigate software issues first. Claude's AH3880 analysis (51.8°C but 30.9% hashrate) demonstrates this principle.

## SECTION 5 — SELF-ASSESSMENT

**Weakest Area - Pattern Recognition Across Fleet:**
My deep dive excelled at individual miner diagnosis but completely missed chip bin correlations, board serial batch patterns, and hardware revision performance gaps. I analyzed trees but missed the forest patterns that predict cascade failures.

**Most Confident and Right - Individual Hardware Failures:**
I correctly identified the critical failure trio (53476, 63940, 64407) and accurately assessed their failure modes. My thermal analysis and restart protocol applications were sound.

**ONE Concrete Change for Tomorrow:**
I will add chip bin and PCB revision data to every miner assessment, creating cohort groupings before individual analysis. This will help identify systematic hardware patterns rather than treating each failure as isolated.

## SECTION 6 — OPEN QUESTIONS

**Disagreement 1 - Fleet Coverage Numbers:**
Claude analyzed 49 miners, I analyzed 33, but rules specify 58 total. Need to determine: Are there miners in different monitoring systems? Are some physically offline but still counted in fleet inventory?

**Disagreement 2 - Miner 53482 Status:**
Claude omitted this miner from predictive warnings despite my identification as a canonical degrader. Need current telemetry data to determine if 53482 has stabilized or if Claude missed a critical failure pattern.

**Disagreement 3 - S21 Economic Thresholds:**
Claude recommended replacing S21 hardware at certain performance levels. Need facility decommission timeline confirmation and hardware replacement cost analysis to determine optimal repair vs. replace thresholds for different hardware generations.

**Disagreement 4 - AH3880 Firmware Timeline:**
Need confirmation of exact firmware versions currently deployed on AH3880 units and whether the April 8 regression rule applies to current symptoms or if this represents a different firmware issue.

The core learning: Claude's weekly synthesis captured fleet-wide patterns I missed in daily granular analysis. My strength is individual miner diagnosis; my weakness is systematic pattern recognition across hardware cohorts.

### SECTION 1 — AGREEMENTS

**Claude agreed with several findings and miner IPs in the deep dive report. These agreements represent HIGH CONFIDENCE areas:**

- **Miner 53476 (Antminer S19JPro):** Both reports identified this miner as completely non-functional, showing no hashrate or temperature readings.
  
- **Miner 53521 (Antminer S19JPro):** Claude's report highlighted multiple warnings and errors related to bad chips on all three hashboards, which aligns with the deep dive findings.

### SECTION 2 — DISAGREEMENTS

#### Disagreement 1:
- **Claude said:** The analysis period from April 7-14 showed widespread hardware degradation across S19J Pro units, indicating end-of-lifecycle behavior rather than environmental issues.
- **You said:** The report focused on specific miners (Miner 53476 and Miner 53521) requiring immediate attention due to significant hardware issues or data reporting problems.
- **Who is right and why?** Claude's broader perspective on systemic hardware degradation is more comprehensive. You missed identifying the pattern of widespread hardware degradation across multiple S19J Pro units, focusing instead on individual miners.
- **Why did you miss it?** The deep dive report was more focused on immediate issues rather than recognizing broader patterns or fleet-wide trends.

#### Disagreement 2:
- **Claude said:** Immediate audit and preemptive replacement for all remaining PCB=0110/BOM=0020 units.
- **You said:** No specific recommendation for auditing PCB/BOM versions.
- **Who is right and why?** Claude's approach of identifying and preemptively replacing problematic hardware batches (PCB=0110/BOM=0020) demonstrates a more proactive stance. You missed this critical pattern recognition.
- **Why did you miss it?** The deep dive report did not correlate the hardware versions with performance metrics, leading to an oversight of systematic failures.

#### Disagreement 3:
- **Claude said:** Restart protocol for S19J Pro units should be modified to attempt PDU cycle first, bypassing firmware restarts for units with >3 previous restart failures.
- **You said:** Recommended investigating and resolving hardware issues (bad chips) affecting Miner 53521 without modifying the restart protocol.
- **Who is right and why?** Claude's recommendation aligns better with the rule that two failed restarts in seven days auto-escalate to RESTART_CHECK_BOARDS. You missed this rule application, leading to less effective recommendations.
- **Why did you miss it?** The deep dive report did not apply the locked operator rules as rigorously, focusing more on immediate hardware issues without considering broader operational protocols.

### SECTION 3 — CLAUDE'S KNOWN ERRORS

1. **"47 of 49 miners online" (actual fleet is 58):**
   - This error violates the rule that the fleet consists of 58 miners, not 49.
   
2. **REPLACE recommendation for s21exphyd/BIXBIT (newest hardware):**

   - This violates rule 8, which states that firmware regression verdicts (like ROLL BACK FIRMWARE) must be followed rather than recommending replacements.

4. **Claude re-proposed the 2-restart escalation rule and 84C threshold rule as "new":**
   - These rules are already locked and should not have been proposed again, indicating a lack of awareness or consideration for existing operational protocols.

5. **Predictive warnings omitted miner 53482 (canonical degrader) and 53476 (new pool code 1003 issue):**
   - Claude's predictive warnings missed identifying these miners as potential failure points, which could have led to proactive maintenance actions.

### SECTION 4 — NEW TECHNIQUES TO INTERNALIZE

- **PCB/BOM Quality Correlation Tracking:** Automatically correlate hardware revision with performance metrics.
  
- **Restart Effectiveness Scoring:** Track success/failure rates by hardware type to optimize intervention strategies.
  
- **Hardware Replacement ROI Calculator:** Cost-benefit analysis for repair vs replace decisions based on hashrate efficiency.

- **Alert Deduplication Counter:** Track how many duplicate alerts are being generated to optimize notification logic.
  
- **Thermal Sensor Health Status:** Flag miners with corrupted temperature telemetry to prevent blind spots in thermal monitoring.

### SECTION 5 — SELF-ASSESSMENT

- The deep dive report was weakest in recognizing broader patterns and fleet-wide trends. It focused more on immediate issues without considering systemic hardware degradation or correlating PCB/BOM versions with performance metrics.

**Most Confident and Right:**

The headline this week is **cascading S19J Pro hardware failures** with 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35 leading a pattern of complete offline events that resist all recovery attempts. These aren't software glitches—they're PSU and control board deaths requiring physical replacement. The fleet's chronic offline rate of 37-50% across S19J Pro cohorts represents a fundamental reliability crisis that firmware restarts cannot fix. Meanwhile, the newer S21 immersion units maintain 99.7% hashrate stability, creating a stark performance divide that validates liquid cooling superiority over aging air-cooled hardware.

## 2. TOP 5 SYSTEMIC ISSUES

### 1. S19J Pro Control Board/PSU Death Cascade (Hardware Quality Pattern)
**Affected Cohorts:** s19jpro/unknown_fw/3/0110/4, s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/unknown_cool, s19jpro/BIXBIT/3/0110/4, s19jpro/unknown_fw/2/0110/4

The local LLM flagged miners 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35 as "offline with no PDU access" across **17 consecutive analyses** from April 12-14. These units show the classic signature of control board or PSU hardware failure: network connectivity lost, no PDU power readings, complete unresponsiveness to firmware restarts. The pattern is spreading—offline events jumped from 216 to 816 across S19J Pro cohorts.

**Action:** Schedule immediate physical inspection of 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35. Prepare replacement PSUs and control boards for the S19J Pro fleet—this failure mode will continue cascading through aging hardware.

### 2. Unknown Firmware Cohorts Show 2x Higher Failure Rates (Firmware Pattern)
**Affected Cohorts:** All "unknown_fw" cohorts averaging 47-84% hashrate vs BiXBiT firmware at 92%+

Cohorts running unknown firmware average 333-632 offline events per week compared to BiXBiT firmware's stable performance. The s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/4 cohort shows 257 offline events across just 3 miners—85+ events per miner weekly. This suggests either corrupted firmware installations or incompatible firmware-hardware combinations.

**Action:** Audit all "unknown_fw" miners for firmware corruption. Standardize on BiXBiT firmware across the S19J Pro fleet where possible, or document which hardware revisions are incompatible.

**Pattern:** Only 2 successful restarts vs 7 documented failures across S19J Pro cohorts

The operator's 20-minute cooldown rule and the new S19J Pro overheating rule reflect learned experience—aging hardware doesn't respond to software fixes. Critical failing miners often show incomplete data collection exactly when diagnostics are most needed. Miner 53481 with its chronic offline events averages only 44% data collection rate, creating blind spots during failure progression.

## 1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The headline this week is **cascading S19J Pro hardware failures** with 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35 leading a pattern of complete offline events that resist all recovery attempts. These aren't software glitches—they're PSU and control board deaths requiring physical replacement. The fleet's chronic offline rate of 37-50% across S19J Pro cohorts represents a fundamental reliability crisis that firmware restarts cannot fix. Meanwhile, the newer S21 immersion units maintain 99.7% hashrate stability, creating a stark performance divide that validates liquid cooling superiority over aging air-cooled hardware.

## 2. TOP 5 SYSTEMIC ISSUES

### 1. S19J Pro Control Board/PSU Death Cascade (Hardware Quality Pattern)
**Affected Cohorts:** s19jpro/unknown_fw/3/0110/4, s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/unknown_cool, s19jpro/BIXBIT/3/0110/4, s19jpro/unknown_fw/2/0110/4

The local LLM flagged miners 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35 as "offline with no PDU access" across **17 consecutive analyses** from April 12-14. These units show the classic signature of control board or PSU hardware failure: network connectivity lost, no PDU power readings, complete unresponsiveness to firmware restarts. The pattern is spreading—offline events jumped from 216 to 816 across S19J Pro cohorts.

**Action:** Schedule immediate physical inspection of 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35. Prepare replacement PSUs and control boards for the S19J Pro fleet—this failure mode will continue cascading through aging hardware.

### 2. Unknown Firmware Cohorts Show 2x Higher Failure Rates (Firmware Pattern)
**Affected Cohorts:** All "unknown_fw" cohorts averaging 47-84% hashrate vs BiXBiT firmware at 92%+

Cohorts running unknown firmware average 333-632 offline events per week compared to BiXBiT firmware's stable performance. The s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/4 cohort shows 257 offline events across just 3 miners—85+ events per miner weekly. This suggests either corrupted firmware installations or incompatible firmware-hardware combinations.

**Action:** Audit all "unknown_fw" miners for firmware corruption. Standardize on BiXBiT firmware across the S19J Pro fleet where possible, or document which hardware revisions are incompatible.

### 3. Restart Effectiveness Breakdown on Aging Hardware (Operator Behavior Pattern)
**Pattern:** Only 2 successful restarts vs 7 documented failures across S19J Pro cohorts

The operator's 20-minute cooldown rule and the new S19J Pro overheating rule reflect learned experience—aging hardware doesn't respond to software fixes. The approval rate for RESTART actions (82 approved, 165 denied) shows operators correctly identifying when hardware failures masquerade as software issues.

**Action:** Implement "hardware age triage" logic: miners >2 years old with hashrate drops get physical inspection priority over restart attempts.

### 4. Chip Bin Quality Correlation Invisible in Current Data (Hardware Quality Gap)
**Pattern:** Bin 3 (58.1% avg HR) vs Bin 1 (75.9% avg HR), but only 1 Bin 1 miner in sample

The cross-miner correlation shows clear chip quality stratification, but the fleet skews heavily toward Bin 2-4 chips. This suggests either poor procurement decisions historically or natural degradation of higher-quality bins over time.

**Action:** Map chip bin quality against miner age and purchase dates. If Bin 1-2 chips are degrading to Bin 3-4 performance, accelerate replacement cycles. If procurement historically favored lower bins, revise purchasing criteria.

### 5. AMS Data Collection Gaps During Critical Failures (Data Quality Issue)
**Pattern:** Miners with 311 readings over 7 days (44% collection rate) vs expected 672 readings

Critical failing miners often show incomplete data collection exactly when diagnostics are most needed. Miner 53481 with its chronic offline events averages only 44% data collection rate, creating blind spots during failure progression.

**Action:** Implement "failure mode data priority"—when a miner drops below 80% hashrate, increase polling frequency and ensure log collection continues even during intermittent connectivity.

These units show the classic signature of control board or PSU hardware failure: network connectivity lost, no PDU power readings, complete unresponsiveness to firmware restarts. The pattern is spreading—offline events jumped from 216 to 816 across S19J Pro cohorts.

**Action:** Schedule immediate physical inspection of 192.168.188.231 and 192.168.188.35. Prepare replacement PSUs and control boards for the S19J Pro fleet—this failure mode will continue cascading through aging hardware.

### 2. Unknown Firmware Cohorts Show 2x Higher Failure Rates (Firmware Pattern)
**Affected Cohorts:** All "unknown_fw" cohorts averaging 47-84% hashrate vs BiXBiT firmware at 92%+

Cohorts running unknown firmware average 333-632 offline events per week compared to BiXBiT firmware's stable performance. The s19jpro/unknown_fw/unknown_chip/unknown_pcb/4 cohort shows 257 offline events across just 3 miners—85+ events per miner weekly. This suggests either corrupted firmware installations or incompatible firmware-hardware combinations.

**Action:** Audit all "unknown_fw" miners for firmware corruption. Standardize on BiXBiT firmware across the S19J Pro fleet where possible, or document which hardware revisions are incompatible.

### 3. Restart Effectiveness Breakdown on Aging Hardware (Operator Behavior Pattern)
**Pattern:** Only 2 successful restarts vs 7 documented failures across S19J Pro cohorts

The operator's 20-minute cooldown rule and the new S19J Pro overheating rule reflect learned experience—aging hardware doesn't respond to software fixes. The approval rate for RESTART actions (82 approved, 165 denied) shows operators correctly identifying when hardware failures masquerade as software issues.

**Action:** Implement "hardware age triage" logic: miners >2 years old with hashrate drops get physical inspection priority over restart attempts.

### 4. Chip Bin Quality Correlation Invisible in Current Data (Hardware Quality Gap)
**Pattern:** Bin 3 (58.1% avg HR) vs Bin 1 (75.9% avg HR), but only 1 Bin 1 miner in sample

The cross-miner correlation shows clear chip quality stratification, but the fleet skews heavily toward Bin 2-4 chips. This suggests either poor procurement decisions historically or natural degradation of higher-quality bins over time.

**Action:** Map chip bin quality against miner age and purchase dates. If Bin 1-2 chips are degrading to Bin 3-4 performance, accelerate replacement cycles. If procurement historically favored lower bins, revise purchasing criteria.

### 5. AMS Data Collection Gaps During Critical Failures (Data Quality Issue)
**Pattern:** Miners with 311 readings over 7 days (44% collection rate) vs expected 672 readings

Critical failing miners often show incomplete data collection exactly when diagnostics are most needed. Miner 53481 with its chronic offline events averages only 44% data collection rate, creating blind spots during failure progression.

**Action:** Implement "failure mode data priority"—when a miner drops below 80% hashrate, increase polling frequency and ensure log collection continues even during intermittent connectivity.
